why: The webhook_run_flow endpoint has no authentication dependency (no Depends(get_current_active_user) or Depends(api_key_security)) in its decorator or parameters; get_webhook_user may allow unauthenticated execution depending on auth settings, exposing flow execution to unauthenticated callers.

The create_upload_file endpoint accepts a file upload and writes it to disk but has no authentication dependency in its decorator or function parameters, allowing any unauthenticated caller to upload files.

raw_code.code is caller-supplied user input passed directly to Component(_code=raw_code.code) and then to build_custom_component_template, which compiles and executes arbitrary Python code without a sandbox or allowlist.

code_request.code is caller-supplied input passed to Component(_code=code_request.code) and build_custom_component_template, enabling execution of arbitrary Python code supplied by the authenticated user.

The build_flow endpoint decorator has no dependencies listed (no Depends(get_current_active_user) in the decorator), and while CurrentActiveUser appears as a parameter, the decorator itself lacks the dependency guard that sibling endpoints use, creating an inconsistency that may allow bypass depending on framework resolution.

The build_public_tmp endpoint is decorated with no auth dependency and no in-body auth check, allowing any unauthenticated caller to trigger flow builds; the only gate is a public-flow DB check which is not equivalent to user authentication.

download_image accepts a caller-supplied flow_id and file_name and serves file content with no authentication dependency or authorization check, allowing any unauthenticated caller to retrieve arbitrary stored images.

The full_file_name is constructed by concatenating a timestamp with the raw user-supplied file.filename without any sanitization or containment check, so a crafted filename (e.g. containing path separators) could cause storage_service.save_file to write outside the intended directory.

The containment check uses str.startswith() on resolved paths, which can be bypassed if allowed_base is a prefix of another directory name (e.g. /base/profile_pictures matches /base/profile_pictures_evil); os.path.commonpath is the safer alternative.
The auto_login endpoint issues long-term access tokens with no authentication dependency; any unauthenticated caller can obtain tokens when AUTO_LOGIN is enabled, bypassing normal credential checks.

exec() is called on a dynamically constructed function body string that interpolates input_.display_name values (from Vertex objects sourced from external flow definitions) directly into the code string without sanitization, allowing arbitrary Python code execution if an attacker can control a vertex display name.

compile() is called on func_body which embeds caller-supplied input_.display_name and flow_id/user_id values into executable Python source code, and the resulting code object is then exec'd without any sandbox or allowlist.

The refresh_token endpoint reads the refresh token directly from a cookie and issues new tokens without any explicit authentication dependency (no Depends(get_current_user) or equivalent), relying solely on the validity of the cookie value passed to create_refresh_token.

order_params['column'] is used with getattr(Flow, ...) to dynamically select a sort column from caller-supplied input; if order_params is passed from external input without strict allowlist validation, an attacker could influence the ORM sort column, and the 'direction' key is similarly dispatched via SORT_DISPATCHER — while not direct SQL string concatenation, this is a logic-level injection risk worth flagging.

Same as unit 18: order_params['column'] drives getattr(Flow, ...) for ORM sort column selection from a caller-supplied dict without a visible allowlist check, risking logic-level injection of arbitrary model attributes.
